ucs.title: [LLM] Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM — IIS w3wp.exe spawning recon & dumping child processes (ProxyLogon-style webshell execution)
ucs.description: Detects post-exploitation child processes spawned by IIS worker (w3wp.exe) running an MSExchange application pool — the canonical signal of a China Chopper / Antsword webshell dropped via Silk Typhoon's Exchange Server 0-day chain (CVE-2021-26855/26857/26858/27065). MSTIC, Volexity and CISA attribute this exact w3wp→cmd→net/whoami/procdump pattern to HAFNIUM during Operation Exchange Marauder, and Microsoft's 2024-25 reporting confirms Silk Typhoon still abuses unpatched Exchange + downstream IIS-hosted apps for initial access.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name="w3wp.exe" (Processes.parent_process="*MSExchange*AppPool*" OR Processes.parent_process="*OWA*" OR Processes.parent_process="*ECP*" OR Processes.parent_process="*Autodiscover*") Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","powershell_ise.exe","pwsh.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","nltest.exe","systeminfo.exe","ipconfig.exe","quser.exe","tasklist.exe","reg.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","procdump.exe","procdump64.exe","rundll32.exe","7z.exe") by host Processes.parent_process Processes.process_name Processes.process Processes.user | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)` | sort - firstTime
ucs.kql:
// Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM — IIS Exchange worker spawning recon/execution children.
// MSTIC ProxyLogon IOC report: w3wp.exe (MSExchange*AppPool) -> cmd/powershell/net/procdump.
let _exchange_app_pools = dynamic(["MSExchangeOWAAppPool","MSExchangeECPAppPool","MSExchangeServicesAppPool","MSExchangeAutodiscoverAppPool","MSExchangeMapiFrontEndAppPool","MSExchangeRpcProxyFrontEndAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellFrontEndAppPool"]);
let _suspicious_children = dynamic(["cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","powershell_ise.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","nltest.exe","systeminfo.exe","ipconfig.exe","quser.exe","tasklist.exe","reg.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","procdump.exe","procdump64.exe","rundll32.exe","7z.exe","7za.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "w3wp.exe"
| where InitiatingProcessCommandLine has_any (_exchange_app_pools)
| where FileName in~ (_suspicious_children)
| where AccountName !endswith "$"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName,
          AppPool = extract(@"(?i)-ap\s+\"?([^\"\s]+)", 1, InitiatingProcessCommandLine),
          ParentCmd = InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          ChildImage = FolderPath,
          ChildCmd = ProcessCommandLine,
          SHA256
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Silk Typhoon HAFNIUM - Exchange IIS Worker Spawns Recon Tooling
id: 7c8a2f51-7d33-4a8f-9a01-silktyphoon-w3wp
status: stable
description: w3wp.exe running an MSExchange application pool spawns recon / execution binaries (cmd, powershell, net, whoami, nltest, procdump). Canonical post-exploitation pattern observed during HAFNIUM Operation Exchange Marauder (CVE-2021-26855 et al.) and re-used by Silk Typhoon in 2024-25 intrusions.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/security/blog/2021/03/02/hafnium-targeting-exchange-servers/
  - https://www.microsoft.com/security/blog/2025/03/05/silk-typhoon-targeting-it-supply-chain/
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/13
tags:
  - attack.initial_access
  - attack.t1190
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.t1505.003
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1059.001
  - detection.silk_typhoon
  - detection.hafnium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\w3wp.exe'
  selection_pool:
    ParentCommandLine|contains:
      - 'MSExchangeOWAAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangeECPAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangeServicesAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangeAutodiscoverAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangeMapiFrontEndAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangeRpcProxyFrontEndAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangePowerShellAppPool'
      - 'MSExchangePowerShellFrontEndAppPool'
  selection_child:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\cmd.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
      - '\net.exe'
      - '\net1.exe'
      - '\whoami.exe'
      - '\nltest.exe'
      - '\systeminfo.exe'
      - '\quser.exe'
      - '\tasklist.exe'
      - '\reg.exe'
      - '\certutil.exe'
      - '\bitsadmin.exe'
      - '\procdump.exe'
      - '\procdump64.exe'
      - '\rundll32.exe'
      - '\7z.exe'
  condition: selection_parent and selection_pool and selection_child
falsepositives:
  - Exchange health / monitoring scripts run from MSExchange app pools (rare, usually scheduled and known)
  - Microsoft-issued mitigation scripts (EOMT.ps1, MitigateExchange) — allowlist by signed-script hash
level: high
ucs.rationale: w3wp.exe is the IIS worker and under normal Exchange operation its children are tightly constrained (CompatTelRunner, conhost for health probes). A child of net.exe / whoami / nltest / procdump under an MSExchange*AppPool parent has effectively zero benign baseline — it requires a remote caller to be issuing OS commands inside the worker process, which is exactly what a ProxyLogon-deployed China C
_sigma_issues: sigma parse error: Sigma rule identifier must be an UUID

ucs.title: [LLM] Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM — LSASS credential dump via rundll32 + comsvcs.dll MiniDump
ucs.description: Detects the comsvcs.dll!MiniDump LOLBin technique used by Silk Typhoon to dump LSASS without dropping ProcDump or Mimikatz. MSTIC's HAFNIUM Exchange Marauder IOC report explicitly lists 'rundll32.exe C:\\windows\\system32\\comsvcs.dll MiniDump <lsass-pid> <outfile> full' as a post-exploitation step after webshell foothold. This is signed-Microsoft-binary credential theft — bare process-name detections will miss it.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name="rundll32.exe" (Processes.process="*comsvcs*MiniDump*" OR Processes.process="*comsvcs*#-?24*" OR Processes.process="*comsvcs.dll*full*") by host Processes.parent_process_name Processes.parent_process Processes.process Processes.user | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)` | sort - firstTime
ucs.kql:
// Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM — LSASS dump via comsvcs.dll!MiniDump (LOLBin).
// MSTIC IOC list explicitly cites: rundll32.exe C:\\windows\\system32\\comsvcs.dll MiniDump <pid> <out> full
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where FileName =~ "rundll32.exe"
| where ProcessCommandLine has "comsvcs"
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("MiniDump","#-?24","#24"," full")
| where AccountName !endswith "$"
| extend TargetPid = extract(@"(?i)comsvcs(?:\.dll)?\s+\S+\s+(\d{2,6})", 1, ProcessCommandLine),
         DumpPath  = extract(@"(?i)comsvcs(?:\.dll)?\s+\S+\s+\d{2,6}\s+(\S+)", 1, ProcessCommandLine)
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName,
          InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          ProcessCommandLine, TargetPid, DumpPath,
          IsInitiatingProcessRemoteSession, SHA256
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
// Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM — comsvcs.dll!MiniDump on Sentinel SecurityEvent 4688.
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where EventID == 4688
| where NewProcessName endswith "\\rundll32.exe"
| where CommandLine has "comsvcs"
| where CommandLine has_any ("MiniDump","#-?24","#24"," full")
| where Account !endswith "$"
| extend TargetPid = extract(@"(?i)comsvcs(?:\.dll)?\s+\S+\s+(\d{2,6})", 1, CommandLine),
         DumpPath  = extract(@"(?i)comsvcs(?:\.dll)?\s+\S+\s+\d{2,6}\s+(\S+)", 1, CommandLine)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, ParentProcessName,
          NewProcessName, CommandLine, TargetPid, DumpPath, TokenElevationType
| order by TimeGenerated desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Silk Typhoon HAFNIUM - LSASS Dump via comsvcs.dll MiniDump
id: 9e1f4c12-1a55-4d6f-b9c0-silktyphoon-comsvcs
status: stable
description: Detects the rundll32 + comsvcs.dll!MiniDump LOLBin used by Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM to dump the LSASS process. Specific command structure is documented in MSTIC's Operation Exchange Marauder IOC release.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/security/blog/2021/03/02/hafnium-targeting-exchange-servers/
  - https://lolbas-project.github.io/lolbas/Libraries/Comsvcs/
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/13
tags:
  - attack.credential_access
  - attack.t1003.001
  - attack.defense_evasion
  - attack.t1218.011
  - detection.silk_typhoon
  - detection.hafnium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_image:
    Image|endswith: '\rundll32.exe'
  selection_cmd:
    CommandLine|contains: 'comsvcs'
  selection_action:
    CommandLine|contains:
      - 'MiniDump'
      - '#-?24'
      - '#24'
      - ' full'
  filter_machine:
    User|endswith: '$'
  condition: selection_image and selection_cmd and selection_action and not filter_machine
falsepositives:
  - Authorised IR forensic collection scripts (very rare, should be allowlisted by hash/host)
level: critical
ucs.rationale: comsvcs.dll!MiniDump is a niche signed-Microsoft-DLL technique with effectively zero legitimate enterprise usage outside red-team / incident-response collection — and the specific argument shape ('MiniDump <pid> <outfile> full' or the ordinal export '#-?24') is uniquely associated with the HAFNIUM IOC release Microsoft published with the ProxyLogon disclosure. It is materially different from procd

_field_issues.message: `IsInitiatingProcessRemoteSession` is not a canonical column of DeviceProcessEvents. Did you mean `InitiatingProcessTokenElevation`?

_sigma_issues: sigma parse error: Sigma rule identifier must be an UUID
